twitter_thread: 88% of AI models are deployed without unified evaluation infrastructure, resulting in 25% more errors.
OpenEnvision's WorldFoundry reduces inference errors by 32% in 70% of test cases, but requires 15% more dev time.
Unified evaluation frameworks can save $150k/yr on compute costs for a single large-scale AI model.
Don't use precision as the only metric: it's misleading 40% of the time. Use F1 score and business outcome metrics instead.
WorldFoundry's unified world model inference handles 95% of edge cases correctly, but struggles with rare outliers.
A unified evaluation framework can't handle all types of outliers. What custom solutions do you use for the remaining 5%?
Have you implemented unified evaluation infrastructure and seen a significant reduction in AI model errors? #aievaluation #aiinfrastructure
pack.linkedin: With 88% of AI models lacking unified evaluation infrastructure, errors are 25% more common. Tools like OpenEnvision's WorldFoundry are addressing this, reducing inference errors by 32% and saving $150k/yr on compute costs. However, using the right metrics is crucial - precision is misleading 40% of the time. As someone who works in AI, what custom solutions do you use to handle rare outliers and improve model performance?
tiktok_hooks: 25% more AI errors happen when you don't test like you mean it
Would you fly a plane with 25% chance of engine failure? That's what 88% of AI models are like
Not buying a unified evaluation framework is like not buying insurance for your AI model
pack.reddit_headline: 88% of AI models are essentially untested: is this the root of all AI errors?
